speaker: interviewer
text: Today is October 27, 1996. We are interviewing a survivor. Henry Binstock. The interviewer is Anula Ellis. We are interviewing in Cleveland, Ohio, United States of America in English. My name is Anula Ellis. I am interviewing Henry Binstock on October 27, 1996 in Cleveland, Ohio, the United States of America. The interview will be in English. What is your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Henry  Binstock.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: H-E-N-R-Y B-I-N-S-T-O-C-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same name. My name at birth is Herszl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: H-E-R-S-Z-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever used any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No other names.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: October 9, 10th year.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 11th year.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: 85 Years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What city were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: What year?

speaker: interviewer
text: What city?

speaker: interviewee
text: What city? Losice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you spell the name of the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: L-O-S-I-C. Losice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what country is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Binstock, let's take you back to Losice. And could you describe for me what life was like when you were growing up there before the war-- before you ever heard of the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a small town. It was about 7,000 Jews lived in Losice in the city. And all the way around, there was the Catholics Christians was living as farmers. And it was a little business in the city. It was storekeepers-- all-- all Jewish people in the city. And it was little shops-- tailors, shoemakers, and Orthodox Jews, rabbis. I used to go in the temple. Saturday was a big holiday and Friday. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: People-- people would come Friday and for Saturday. You know, they-- all went together in the temple. It was  a little religious, Jewish little city. And there was-- it was a public school. There were private schools for the children like cheders. You know? And there-- that was the life. It was not-- there was no electricity in the beginning when I was born. Then later, they started to build up the city with-- with electricity and used to make theaters.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- they built a theater. And the-- was organizations. There was a Zionist organization. And there was a organization with workers. And they are more in the leftist side-- it was a little on the Communist side, there were poor people. And-- and it was-- it was really a poor-- a poor little town of poor people. But later on, they started to build up with the businesses. And in my family, it was-- we started to make shoes for a shoe store. And shoe stores-- we got salesmen on the road, too. And there was-- it was one-- one big family-- three brothers. We used to go-- we got workers. We used to work and make a lot of shoes for-- for stores. And this-- the salesmen used to send us the orders. And-- this was before the war. And then-- and we got-- we-- the businesses started getting bigger. And we used to send for the shoes-- when the-- other towns, and the other cities. And then when the war broke out, we-- we-- the-- the last day when the war broke out, this was in-- in September the 1st. It was broke out the war. And the same day, we sent away a lot of shoes-- about-- about 1,000 pair of shoes in the-- in different areas.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me take you back just a little bit to before the war broke out to what your family life was like.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you talk about your mother, and your father, and your sisters, and brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My mother was with-- they-- we-- we didn't have in that time before the war, we didn't have refrigerators. We didn't have-- we didn't have toilets in the house. We didn't have water in the house. I had to go far away to-- to bring water. And they had to make fire. And, you know, it was not stoves to build up the ovens with bricks. If they built up a stove, they have to make fire from wood. And so it was a hard life with cooking-- with-- with running the house for the children-- for the household. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the-- the-- my father used to go-- he was a little bit religious. He used to go every morning in the temple to pray. And then-- and then this was the life. It was a hard life. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Avrum-- Abraham.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Fejga.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell your mother's and your father's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name is Feja is F-E-J-G-A. Fejga. And Avrum was A-V-R-U-M.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have sisters and brothers? Sisters and brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Brothers? My older brother was Berl. And the younger brother-- my sister-- my younger brother was Jeshua. And then I got the sisters-- Rivka, and Chaja, Esther, and Bayla, and Sarah. That's five sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. How many of you worked in the family business with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we was-- the whole family was in the business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the whole family was working in their only business together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the business in your home? Or was it in a separate building?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was home. It was home. And we used to give out a lot of work outside other workers. And they used to take it in the morning. There were-- in the evening, they'll bring it back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any outside workers coming to your home to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Outside of the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Outside, yeah, used to-- used to be about four or five workers in our house besides us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many people worked for your father by the time the war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was working about-- about 12 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Outside of the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then [INTERPOSING VOICES] all your sisters and brothers as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your mother participate in this business? What did she do?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she was running the house for the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have any household help? Any household help?

speaker: interviewee
text: Household, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live in a house or in an apartment? Could you describe that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a house. It was a big house. It was-- three families were living in that house. It was three-- three separate suites.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe what it looked like-- what your suite looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- there was a kitchen. And it was-- a bedroom, in the front room, it was together. And then we got a special shop another room, was working-- people was working-- about-- about 10-- about 10 people, 10, 12 people was working the shop.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did all the children sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were sleeping in one room. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother and father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two sisters-- two sisters were sleeping in one-- one bed, and two brothers in a separate bed, and the rest-- the rest of the children on a different bed, and the father and the mother were separate.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a separate room?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it wasn't a separate.

speaker: interviewer
text: The same room?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Very different. What was your job for the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my-- my job in the shop?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was just working making shoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to school. I went in the public school for a couple years before I  went to a cheder. I went to private school. Then I went a couple years in the public school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any hobbies when you were a youngster?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I got a lot of hobbies. I would like-- I like always to fix things. And when we got in the-- in our shop the electricity, and like anything that happens to the electricity I was fixing myself. I was-- I liked to do things. I was-- I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Any other interests? Did you have any musical abilities?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you play any instruments?

speaker: interviewee
text: No radio, no nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you play any musical instruments?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was playing in-- the instrument was a mandolin-- you hear about it. It's like-- rounded like a  guitar. You know? That's what I was playing myself-- I was trying to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you still do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't do it for a long, long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you enjoy singing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was-- I was singing in a group. And I was-- and we got-- our organization was a Zionist organization. We used to play-- we-- we played-- I got even a group in Israel where our people now in our hometown was-- is-- written a book many years ago. And I got that book in Yiddish. But they were writing about me. I was in the top players. It was all kind of these theaters we used to make with their names maybe. But it was in theaters. And I used to participate in the theaters.

speaker: interviewer
text: As an actor or as a musician?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was-- I was acting.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really? That's--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I once-- I played-- I played in a   play. It was from the Zionist organization. If he wrote it about a big-- this officer that was in the Jewish-- in the Jewish Army-- he was-- he was-- the-- the Arabs killed him-- by the name Trumpeldor. Have you heard about Trumpeldor? A big officer. He was-- he was in the English Army with-- with one hand. He's got-- he lost an arm in the English Army in Israel. And he was from the first-- the first kibbutzim-- first kibbutz in Israel. And they played-- played a play about him about that time. And I played the-- his role. I played-- I played Trumpeldor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that professional? Or was that as a hobby that you were acting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- it was-- I belonged to a club, where they was playing often different kinds of plays.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I understood that you also sang.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was wondering if you could bring back a song that might have been forgotten and just sing a phrase or two from it.

speaker: interviewee
text: This I play-- I sing in the-- in the Jewish-- in the Jewish-- in the Jewish organization here in Cleveland. I was singing there. I even got a picture from them when I was singing in a group.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to sing just a little bit of a song to bring back some music from a time that's now lost?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I don't have anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did you have any other particular interests or hobbies when you were young?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hobbies? What are--

speaker: interviewer
text: Like, did you date girls? Did you dance? That sort of thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can dance. I was-- I was running-- I was with another friend in Losice. We got this dance group, was learning-- learning people dancing. Yeah, I was a good dancer, but [LAUGHS] not now. I can dance. But not--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have an opportunity to travel away from your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I traveled away. And one time before when my father started to launch the business with salesmen, I went to kibbutz in Bialystok in Poland. I was three months in the kibbutz. And I was trying to get a certificate to go to Israel at that time. And it was-- I had to be there too long. It was my friend who was there for three years. And it's got a hard time to get a certificate. And so I left the kibbutz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you give up your dream of going to Palestine then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't give up. I was trying to go. But it was no way-- not-- then when the war broke out, it was-- was a waste.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall having any non-Jewish friends or neighbors in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: My friends? Oh, I got a lot of friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any non-Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too many non-Jewish friends-- not in our city.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the-- how would you describe the relationships between Christians and Jews in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- in our city was real bad. We didn't-- we didn't like each other. But in the school, in the-- in the public school-- they used to go together. You know? But it was not too friendly, because it was the anti-Semitic in that time. It was-- was too much, anti-Semitic there, especially the government. It was-- the main general in Poland was his name, Pilsudski. He was a general. And he was protecting the Jews, because in the war when-- when Pilsudski-- Polish people took over from Russia, Poland in the Second World War-- in the First World War, I mean.

speaker: interviewee
text: So Pilsudski didn't trust his own people. And he used to-- when-- when they looked for him, the-- the-- the Russians at the time, the-- the Communists took over in the First World War. So he was hiding Jews only. He came here once to-- he was hiding once by a Jew. And they came in to look for him. The Jew put him in a tallis and tefillin. [LAUGHS] And he was saying a prayer. You know? So-- so he was-- was protecting the Jews all these-- all these years until he died in Poland. How did you know about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- it was the story. They were writing in the papers about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was-- was this common knowledge?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was written in the papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you first notice that the life you had been living till then started changing?

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS] What?

speaker: interviewer
text: That the war was coming-- when did things start changing?

speaker: interviewee
text: When they started changing? Well, the war broke out. So we-- after the war was changing-- when war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- the war broke out in-- in September the 1st.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1939. The war broke out. And-- and the people-- the people started to run around. They didn't know what to do. You know, they came in our town, our city, came from Warsaw-- from all over the country [? was ?] [? came ?] people hiding. And they think this is a good hiding place. But the Germans came in. First-- the first thing that they-- that they bombed right away our city in-- in-- in September the  1st.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you when the bombs came?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we-- the whole family-- the whole family-- our family went in the forest. It was not far from our city and was hiding. And because-- because we knew it was going to be bombing. And we started to go away there, walk away, and the bombs started to fly. A few things-- they had broken up the-- the synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see the synagogue bombed?

speaker: interviewee
text: When-- when he started to walk away, the same street in the synagogue was-- was still close. They smashed up the synagogue right-- right at the first day.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We--

speaker: interviewer
text: As you were leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: We couldn't [LAUGHS] look back. They have to run. So we was there a couple days in the-- in the city-- in the forest. And then-- then on the second day in the-- in the evening, in the middle of the night, we came to get something from the house. It was the house. It was close, not far from the temple. And the house was smashed all to pieces. So we started to get in and get something out. And we went away while it's dark.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to get any of your possessions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we just looking for food. We-- we put it in the clothes. We ran away with the best clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you lived in the forest, can you describe where you stayed for those few days?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, just there was trees. Was-- was laying on the ground a few days. And then when it's-- when it's everything-- you already quieted down. The Germans came in. And we came back the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the Germans there when you came back? Were the Germans in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you encounter any of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we-- we saw them. They were in the city. It was-- they were going around coming and going out. So the army was going to get through. So in the meantime, they was-- they was there about a week or two in the-- in the city. And then they started coming in close-- the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Russians were coming closer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So finally, the-- the Russians came in. And they moved out the Germans. And then the Russians were maybe about-- about three or four weeks in the city. And then-- then we found out as they started the people to talk are the Russians going to go away, because they got a deal with the-- with the Germans just to go to the River Bug. And it's not far-- the Bug was there. So when they-- then they started to go back, the Russians, and the Germans came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans came back into your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: While all this was happening, where did you and your family stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we was-- we was in the city. We were hanging around the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we lived close in that house-- in that house, not far. The house was not demolished completely. But we were staying around in the meantime.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a different house or your own house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was-- was close, that house. So in the meantime, the-- the-- the Russians started to move out. So we decided to move out too. So we took out-- we-- we hired a horse and buggy. We-- we put it in-- we put it in our-- this-- we got a lot of shoes still. We took it out from that house and put it in. And we went over the Bug.

speaker: interviewer
text: We took the horse and buggy-- there is to go a platform on the border, you know? A lot of-- a lot of vagrants used to go over the border. And we were sitting there in the first city. It was-- the name, it was Siemiatycze. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And from there, Siemiatycze-- I don't know if you got it marked down. And from Siemiatycze, we-- we took a train. And we went to Lida then Vilnius. We had a lot of customers in Lida. And we came there. And we was-- we was there a nice couple months. And then the-- the Russians were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in one-- one night, they took a lot of the refugees. And they packed them in-- in the-- in the trains where they-- where they-- the cattle trains. And they sent them away to Siberia. And it was my older brother Bill with his family. And they got in-- in some streets, other refugees were there. Like we, our family, they didn't even touch it. So later on, a couple weeks later, the Russians says, hey, you can't-- everybody have to go from here-- it is the refugees. So they made us to go to-- if you heard about Rovno, a big city in Poland, Rovno, and they-- they sent us in a little city next to Rovno. The name of it was Kostopil. It became then Kostopil. And we found a little-- a little house. You know? [INTERPOSING VOICES] How did you get there? What was the transport?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and we was there on that-- we found a little house. And we lived there for a couple months. It was Ukraine. It was Poland. It was Ukrainian side. And then when-- when the Germans was very close, then they attacked Russia. And they attacked. And it was close already to Rovno. So-- so I-- I decided to go to Russia-- walk away. And my parents. They were still there-- the whole family. [DRINKS] My sister-- one sister Chaja, she wants to go with us. My mother didn't let her.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went away with my younger brother, by the way, Jeshua. By the way. So we went to Russia. And we walked in a-- in different shops making shoes. And first-- first we came to-- many times they arrested us in Russia because we was wearing Polish clothing. And any-- any person, any kids who saw us in the street, they right away called the police. We came to Kiev. Have you heard about Kiev? We came to Kiev. I got a cousin in Kiev from the First World War. And I knew the address. And I went to him to see him. So he was-- he was in the shoe business too. He worked in a government shop. And-- and that-- we came in with a lot of refugees in a square. It was that square we was holding our packages. We left with our friends. And we went to a street called [INAUDIBLE]. I know the name from the-- from the house. And we came there. And the-- and in that way we came. Until we came there, we were arrested twice. What-- where were you taken when you were arrested?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kiev.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where-- OK, when you were arrested, where did they take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Kiev. They arrested-- the police, but they let us out right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever in jail incarcerated?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [INAUDIBLE] So then we came there. And he was not there. He was working night shift. So his wife recognized me. She said I looked like her husband. [LAUGHS] So she knew right who I am. Anyway, we had to go back to the square. And we-- we have to come back later. In a couple hours, she was gonna be back from work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Finally, we came-- we came back. And we saw him. And they made us a nice meal, you know? We were sitting with them together. And anyway, he talked us into-- into enlisting in the Russian Army. I said, we're not gonna do that. So in the-- so anyway, he was anxious as to do that. I said, you're not going to do it. Finally, we came back. And on the way, they arrested us again. They took us in-- in a-- under this fence. And they want us-- and they wanted us, you know, to-- to show the papers-- passports.

speaker: interviewee
text: And finally, I see I went to-- [? and you ?] see, we were stuck there. So anyway, I saw a table where there was sitting a Jewish-- Jewish officer. I went to  him. And I said, I got some packages in the square. And I want-- I want to get out. And I wanted my passport. He said-- he said, he wanted to help us. He said, I'll give it to you. But you have to come back. If you don't come back, you're going to be in big trouble.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went out. We didn't come back. So we went away. And then we walked in shops, in different shops. And finally, they sent us in a labor camp and that labor camp in Stalingrad. You hear about Stalingrad? When the Germans-- the Germans got-- lost the war there. So we have to work to make a road for the train to go around the Germans eight miles behind them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was there. And I-- I got a [INAUDIBLE] there. And they put me into fix the shoes for the people-- thousands of people. So in-- on one side, it was the biggest [? war there. ?] One one side, it was Germans there-- 5,000, 6,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: We are going to stop now. And we're going to change tapes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Change?
